title: Roger Penrose — ORCH-OR and Non-Computable Physics (Collected Papers)
subtitle: 6 papers on quantum state reduction, the non-computable, twistor theory, and the Diósi-Penrose criterion — the physical basis of śaktipāta and the 38th principle

summary.one_line: Penrose's ORCH-OR theory of quantum state reduction as a non-computable physical process provides the physical basis for śaktipāta (grace) — the variable in the system that cannot be engineered, th...
summary.scope: Covers 6 papers (2018-2025): the Diósi-Penrose criterion for quantum gravity induced collapse, twistor theory, the non-computable, the quantum mind, and consciousness-related physics.

sources.label: Roger Penrose et al. — Collected Papers (2018-2025). 6 PDFs in /root/projects/blog/science/authors/penrose/. ArXiv IDs: 1812.04630 through 2509.01586.

body.text: Penrose argues that objective quantum state reduction (OR) is a non-computable physical process — not random (as in standard QM) and not algorithmic. The moment of reduction cannot be predicted from prior states. This IS śaktipāta — grace as the non-computable gap in the fabric of reality. The five degrees of grace (tīvratīvra → manda) correspond to five degrees of non-computability: from complete openness to almost-complete determination. Penrose gives the 38th principle a physical basis: the non-computable IS the Anākhyā — the aspect of reality that cannot be captured by any formal system.

Twistor theory replaces space-time points with twistors — complex geometric objects that encode both location and momentum. Space-time emerges from twistor interactions. This IS the paśyantī level of speech: the geometry before space-time, where sound (information) and light (geometry) are undifferentiated. The twistor IS the phoneme at the paśyantī level — the unit of reality that is both information and form.

gaps: Full extraction of all 6 papers needed

issues.title: Full corpus extraction needed
issues.description: 6 papers collected but not fully extracted.

versions.summary: Initial RO for Penrose's collected papers. 2 passages mapping non-computable to grace, twistor to paśyantī.
